cwe: CWE-639 - Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

threat.title: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) in REST API Endpoints
threat.description: IDOR vulnerabilities occur when APIs expose direct references to internal implementation objects (like database keys or file paths) without proper authorization checks. Attackers can manipulate these references to access unauthorized data belonging to other users. This is the #1 API security risk according to OWASP, present in approximately 40% of all API attacks and representing a $1 billion+ annual impact across the security industry.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker authenticates as legitimate user (userID: 123) with valid credentials
2. Discovers API endpoint pattern: GET /api/users/123/profile returns their own profile data
3. Changes userID parameter to 124: GET /api/users/124/profile
4. API fails to verify if authenticated user (123) should access user 124's data
5. Receives sensitive information: email, phone number, address, order history of user 124
6. Automates enumeration: Iterates through IDs 1-10000 to harvest thousands of user records
7. Exfiltrates personally identifiable information (PII) for identity theft or resale
prerequisites: API uses predictable object identifiers (sequential integers, GUIDs without authorization)
Application fails to verify user authorization at object level
User can authenticate with at least low-privilege account
API endpoints accept user-controllable object references (IDs in URL path or query parameters)

cve_references.description: IDOR in One Identity Identity Manager 9.x before 9.3 allows privilege escalation through insecure direct object reference, affecting On-Premise installations
IDOR in Bagisto v.1.5.1 e-commerce platform allows attackers to obtain sensitive invoice information via invoice ID parameter manipulation
IDOR vulnerability in Moodle Course Badges allowing unauthorized access to badge information through ID manipulation

real_world_breaches.incident: OWASP API Security Top 10 - BOLA #1 Risk
real_world_breaches.impact: 40% of all API attacks involve BOLA/IDOR vulnerabilities, making it the most prevalent API security risk worldwide
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Missing object-level authorization checks in API implementations across thousands of organizations

real_world_breaches.incident: TikTok Memories Privacy Breach via IDOR
real_world_breaches.impact: Researcher discovered IDOR allowing viewing of other users' private 'Memories' by manipulating aweme_id parameter, potentially exposing millions of private videos
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Inadequate authorization checks on memory/video access API endpoints

real_world_breaches.impact: Over 200 IDOR vulnerabilities reported monthly on HackerOne platform; 15% of retail/ecommerce bounties paid for IDOR, 18% government, 36% medical technology, 31% professional services
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Systemic failure to implement object-level authorization across industries

bug_bounty_reports.summary: IDOR vulnerability in HackerOne platform allowing deletion of licenses and certifications via GraphQL query manipulation
IDOR in Affirm platform to view order information of other users including delivery addresses and purchase history
IDOR when editing email in Automattic platform leads to account takeover by modifying user_id parameter

security_research.title: OWASP API Security Top 10 - API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization
security_research.key_finding: BOLA ranked #1 API security risk in 2023, affecting approximately 40% of API attacks. Authorization checks must be performed for every API request accessing sensitive objects.

security_research.title: IDOR: The #1 Vulnerability Found by Penetration Testers in 2023
security_research.key_finding: IDOR identified as most common vulnerability in penetration testing engagements, with consistent exploitation patterns across industries

security_research.title: IDOR in 2025: Why Broken Access Control Still Rules the Vulnerability Charts
security_research.key_finding: IDOR continues to dominate vulnerability statistics in 2025 with real-world demonstrations showing systematic failures across modern API implementations

mitigations.title: Implement Object-Level Authorization Checks
mitigations.description: Enforce authorization checks at the object level for every API request. Verify that the authenticated user has permission to perform the requested action on the specific object, not just access to the endpoint. This is the most critical defense against IDOR attacks.
validation_steps: Test accessing /api/users/124 as user 123 (should return 403 Forbidden)
Verify admin users can access all user profiles
Test with invalid/non-existent object IDs (should return 404)
Verify authorization checks occur before database queries

mitigations.title: Use UUIDs Instead of Sequential Integer IDs
mitigations.description: Replace sequential, predictable integer IDs with Universally Unique Identifiers (UUIDs) or cryptographically random tokens. This makes object enumeration computationally infeasible while adding defense-in-depth. Note: This is NOT a substitute for proper authorization checks.
validation_steps: Verify all new records use UUID/random tokens as identifiers
Test that sequential ID enumeration is no longer possible
Confirm UUID validation prevents malformed input
Verify authorization checks still enforce access control
Update API documentation to reflect UUID format

mitigations.title: Implement Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)
mitigations.description: Move beyond simple ownership checks to policy-based access control that evaluates user attributes, resource attributes, environmental conditions, and actions. ABAC provides fine-grained authorization suitable for complex multi-tenant and enterprise applications.
iso27001_controls: A.9.2.3 - Management of privileged access rights
nist_csf: PR.AC-4 - Access permissions are managed, incorporating principles of least privilege and separation of duties
PR.PT-3 - Access to systems and assets is controlled
validation_steps: Define comprehensive authorization policies for all user roles
Test that users can only access resources based on attribute rules
Verify managers can access their team's resources
Test policy changes don't require code deployment
Verify authorization decisions are logged for audit

indicators: Multiple 403 Forbidden responses from same IP address within short timeframe
High volume of API requests to single endpoint with varying ID parameters
Access to objects created by different users from single authenticated session
API requests with IDs outside normal user's resource range
Sudden spike in 404 Not Found errors (attacker probing for valid IDs)
Access patterns inconsistent with normal user behavior (e.g., customer accessing admin resources)

log_queries.query:
// Detect IDOR enumeration attempts via sequential ID access
AppServiceHTTPLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| where CsUriStem matches regex @"/api/\w+/\d+"
| extend ResourceID = extract(@"/(\d+)", 1, CsUriStem)
| extend Endpoint = extract(@"(/api/\w+)/", 1, CsUriStem)
| summarize 
    RequestCount = count(),
    UniqueIDs = dcount(ResourceID),
    IDList = make_set(ResourceID),
    StatusCodes = make_set(ScStatus)
    by CIp, Endpoint, CsUsername, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where UniqueIDs > 20  // More than 20 different IDs in 5 minutes
| extend Suspicious = case(
    RequestCount > 100, "High",
    UniqueIDs > 50, "High",
    true, "Medium"
  )
| project TimeGenerated, CIp, CsUsername, Endpoint, RequestCount, UniqueIDs, Suspicious, StatusCodes
| order by RequestCount desc

index=api_logs sourcetype=rest_api
| rex field=uri_path "/api/(?<endpoint>\w+)/(?<resource_id>\d+)"
| where isnotnull(resource_id)
| eventstats dc(resource_id) as unique_ids by src_ip, endpoint, user_id
| where unique_ids > 20
| stats 
    count as request_count,
    dc(resource_id) as unique_resources,
    values(status_code) as status_codes,
    earliest(_time) as first_seen,
    latest(_time) as last_seen
    by src_ip, user_id, endpoint
| eval duration=tostring(last_seen-first_seen, "duration")
| where request_count > 50 OR unique_resources > 30
| eval severity=case(
    request_count > 200, "critical",
    unique_resources > 100, "critical",
    request_count > 100, "high",
    true, "medium"
  )
| table first_seen, src_ip, user_id, endpoint, request_count, unique_resources, duration, status_codes, severity
| sort -request_count

sequence by source.ip, user.id with maxspan=10m
  [api where event.category == "web" and http.response.status_code == 200 and url.path : "/api/*/[0-9]*"]
  [api where event.category == "web" and http.response.status_code == 200 and url.path : "/api/*/[0-9]*"]
  [api where event.category == "web" and http.response.status_code == 200 and url.path : "/api/*/[0-9]*"]
  [api where event.category == "web" and http.response.status_code == 200 and url.path : "/api/*/[0-9]*"]
  [api where event.category == "web" and http.response.status_code == 200 and url.path : "/api/*/[0-9]*"]

fields @timestamp, @message, request.url, request.ip, user.id, response.statusCode
| filter request.url like /\/api\/\w+\/\d+/
| parse request.url /\/api\/(?<endpoint>\w+)\/(?<resourceId>\d+)/
| stats count() as requestCount, count_distinct(resourceId) as uniqueResources by request.ip, user.id, endpoint, bin(5m)
| filter uniqueResources > 20 or requestCount > 100
| sort requestCount desc

resource.type="api"
jsonPayload.request.url=~"/api/.*/[0-9]+"
timestamp>="2026-01-30T00:00:00Z"
| group_by [jsonPayload.request.ip, jsonPayload.user.id, jsonPayload.request.endpoint], 
    count(),
    count_distinct(jsonPayload.request.resourceId),
    window(5m)
| having count() > 100 OR count_distinct(jsonPayload.request.resourceId) > 30

tools.name: Wazuh
tools.usage: Configure custom decoders and rules to detect IDOR patterns in API logs

tools.name: Falco
tools.usage: falco -r idor_detection_rules.yaml # Monitor syscalls and API activity for IDOR attempts

tools.usage: Automated IDOR detection during security testing with Active Scan rules
